content.problemStatement:
Little chef has just been introduced to the world of numbers! While experimenting with addition and multiplication operations, the little chef came up with the following problem:



 Given an array **A** of non-negative integers, how many pairs of indices **i** and **j** exist such that ** A[i]*A[j] > A[i]+A[j] ** where ** i < j **.


 Now being a learner, little chef isn't able to solve this problem efficiently and hence turns to you for help.
content.inputFormat: First line contains T, the number of test cases. Each test case starts with an integer N, the number of elements in the array A. The next line contains N non-negative integers, the elements of array A.
content.outputFormat: For each test case, print the number of pairs of indices i and j such that A[i]*A[j] > A[i]+A[j] and i < j.

sampleTestCases.explanation: For the first test case, the pairs (3, 4), (3, 5), and (4, 5) satisfy the condition. For the second test case, no pairs satisfy the condition.
The pairs (2, 3), (2, 4), (2, 5), (3, 4), (3, 5), and (4, 5) satisfy the condition.

solution.approach: The problem can be solved by iterating over all pairs of indices i and j in the array A, and checking if the condition A[i]*A[j] > A[i]+A[j] is satisfied. If it is, increment the count of pairs. The time complexity of this approach is O(N^2) per test case, where N is the number of elements in the array A.
solution.timeComplexity: O(N^2) per test case, O(T*N^2) total.
solution.spaceComplexity: O(1) — the space used does not grow with the size of the input, only a constant amount of space is used to store the count and the input array.
